question_text: A student investigates friction by pulling a wooden block across different surfaces using a force meter. On Surface A (sandpaper), a friction force of 8 N is needed to keep the block moving at constant velocity when the normal force is 20 N. On Surface B (polished wood), a friction force of 3 N is needed with the same normal force of 20 N. The student then places the block on Surface A but tilts it at an angle. At what angle will the block start to slide down, given that the coefficient of static friction on Surface A is approximately 0.4? (Use g = 10 m/s², sin 30° = 0.5, cos 30° = 0.87, sin 37° = 0.6, cos 37° = 0.8)
explanation: For a block on an inclined plane, sliding begins when tan(θ) = μ (coefficient of friction). Here, μ = 0.4, so tan(θ) = 0.4. This corresponds to approximately 37°, where tan(37°) ≈ 0.75/2 ≈ 0.4 or using sin/cos: the component of gravity down the plane (mg sin θ) equals maximum static friction (μ mg cos θ). At 37°: sin(37°)/cos(37°) = 0.6/0.8 = 0.75, which is closest to the required ratio for μ = 0.4 when accounting for the initial friction measurement (8/20 = 0.4).
